title: The Schwarzschild metric: an exact line element from the Eastern Front
intro: Einstein presented the final form of general relativity to the Prussian Academy on November 25, 1915, and acknowledged in the same paper that his field equations admitted exact solutions only in idealized cases. Six weeks later a letter arrived from the Eastern Front. Karl Schwarzschild, computing artillery range tables for the German army between Russian shellings, had solved the vacuum equations outside a static spherical mass in closed form. The line element he posted is the gravitational field outside every spherical star, planet, and non-rotating black hole, controlled by a single mass parameter and exhibiting three physical regimes (weak field, photon sphere, event horizon) that this lesson walks through in turn.

sections.heading: 1. Karl Schwarzschild, an artillery lieutenant on the Russian front

blocks.text: Karl Schwarzschild, age 42, was director of the Potsdam astrophysical observatory and one of the most respected mathematician-astronomers in Germany when he volunteered for the army in August 1914. He served as a meteorologist in Belgium during 1914, then as a lieutenant in the artillery on the Eastern Front through 1915, computing range tables that adjusted shell trajectories for wind, air density, and the rotation of the Earth. His unit was in the Polish-Russian borderlands when the November 1915 issue of the Prussian Academy proceedings reached the trenches with Einstein's just-completed general theory of relativity.

Schwarzschild read the four November papers and saw immediately that Einstein had a problem. The field equations were nonlinear in the metric, and Einstein's own perihelion calculation in the third paper had used a perturbative expansion whose convergence nobody had checked. Within three weeks Schwarzschild had a closed-form exact solution for the vacuum outside a spherical mass, posted from the front on December 22, 1915. Einstein received the manuscript on January 13, 1916, replied the same day that 'I had not expected that the exact solution to the problem could be formulated so simply,' and presented it to the Academy on Schwarzschild's behalf on January 16.

The cover letter quoted Goethe's Faust on how war can leave room for 'a stroll through the land of your ideas,' and contained a Latin tag, vivat sequens (long live the sequel), pointing Einstein at a second paper Schwarzschild had already written giving the metric inside a uniform-density sphere. Schwarzschild submitted the interior paper in February 1916. In March he contracted pemphigus, a rare autoimmune blistering disease the trenches probably accelerated, and the army sent him home. He died at the Potsdam observatory on May 11, 1916. The two papers from the Eastern Front, written in five months on the war's edge, are the foundation of the relativistic theory of stars and black holes.

The story has the hazard of being too good to be true, and the relief of being fully documented. The Faust quote sits in the January 22 letter to Einstein, the artillery service is in his army records, the death from pemphigus is in the Potsdam obituaries. What follows is the exterior solution he wrote down: what assumptions he made, what the line element looks like, what Mercury's orbit said about it, and how it took the field another 44 years to read the formula's most famous radius correctly.

sections.heading: 2. Vacuum, spherical symmetry, the static ansatz

blocks.text: Three assumptions reduce Einstein's full ten-equation system to a tractable pair of ordinary differential equations.

First, vacuum. Einstein's field equations are G_μν = (8πG/c⁴) T_μν, where G_μν is the Einstein tensor (a particular combination of the Ricci tensor R_μν = R^ρ_μρν from the previous lesson with the metric g_μν, built to satisfy ∇^μ G_μν = 0 identically) and T_μν is the stress-energy tensor that packages local energy density, momentum density, and stress into a single 4×4 symmetric matrix. In a region of empty space, T_μν vanishes identically, which forces G_μν = 0 and, by tracing, R_μν = 0.

blocks.caption: R_μν is the Ricci tensor, the symmetric 4×4 contraction of the Riemann curvature tensor introduced in the previous lesson, R_μν = R^ρ_μρν. The indices μ and ν each run over the four spacetime coordinates (t, r, θ, φ). The vanishing condition R_μν = 0 is the vacuum form of Einstein's field equations, valid wherever the stress-energy tensor T_μν is zero. The Schwarzschild solution describes the spacetime outside a spherical star or planet, where this condition holds; it says nothing about the interior, which requires T_μν ≠ 0 and is the subject of Schwarzschild's second 1916 paper.

blocks.text: Second, spherical symmetry. The metric is invariant under all rotations around a central point. In adapted coordinates (t, r, θ, φ) the angular part of the line element is forced to the standard form r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²), leaving only two unknown radial functions in the (t, r) part. Third, the static ansatz: the metric components depend on r alone (no t-dependence) and contain no dt-dr cross term (no radial flow). Combined, the spherically-symmetric static line element reduces to two unknown radial functions A(r) and B(r).

blocks.tex: ds^2 = -A(r)\, c^2\, dt^2 + B(r)\, dr^2 + r^2 (d\theta^2 + \sin^2\!\theta\, d\varphi^2)
blocks.caption: ds² is the squared spacetime interval. A(r) is the unknown radial function multiplying the time-time component, with the sign convention -A(r) so that A(r) > 0 for the timelike direction in the (−,+,+,+) signature. B(r) is the unknown radial function multiplying the radial-radial component, with B(r) > 0. c is the speed of light. t is the time coordinate of a distant inertial observer. r is the areal radial coordinate, defined so that the surface r = const has area 4πr². θ and φ are the standard polar and azimuthal angles inherited from spherical symmetry. The angular part r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²) is forced by the symmetry; only A(r) and B(r) remain to be determined.

blocks.text: Substituting this ansatz into R_μν = 0 produces a small system whose two independent equations, combined with the boundary condition that the metric approach Minkowski at infinity, has a unique solution. Garrett Birkhoff sharpened the picture in 1923 by proving the static assumption is redundant: any spherically symmetric vacuum solution to Einstein's equations is automatically static, even if the source pulsates. The result is the relativistic generalisation of Newton's 1687 shell theorem (which proved that the gravitational field outside a spherical Newtonian source equals that of a point mass at the center). The next section writes down the unique solution.

quiz.question: A spherically symmetric star undergoes radial pulsations, expanding and contracting by 1% in radius. The pulsation preserves spherical symmetry at every moment but is obviously time-dependent. What does a distant observer measure for the gravitational field outside the star?
options: A time-varying field whose pulsations propagate outward as spherically-symmetric gravitational waves at the speed of light.

A static Schwarzschild field, with the time variations of the source completely invisible outside it. Birkhoff's theorem (1923) shows that any spherically symmetric vacuum solution of Einstein's equations is automatically static, and gravitational radiation requires at minimum a time-varying quadrupole moment that radial pulsations do not have.
A static Schwarzschild field, but only in the weak-field limit; for a relativistic compact object the pulsation would leak out.
A pulsating field whose amplitude falls as 1/r², matching the Newtonian shell theorem in the slow-motion limit.
quiz.explanation: Birkhoff's theorem is the relativistic generalisation of Newton's 1687 shell theorem. It states that the only spherically symmetric vacuum solution to Einstein's equations is the static Schwarzschild solution, regardless of any time dependence assumed inside the source. A radial pulsation has no quadrupole moment by symmetry, and gravitational radiation requires at minimum a changing quadrupole, so no waves escape. Option (a) gets the geometry wrong: the exterior is static even when the source is not. Option (c) is wrong because Birkhoff's theorem holds for the full nonlinear equations, not just the weak-field limit; the only escape clause is to drop spherical symmetry. Option (d) confuses Newtonian with relativistic shell theorems and would imply spherically symmetric gravitational waves, which the spin-2 polarisation structure of linearised gravity rules out independently of Birkhoff.

sections.heading: 3. The line element, the Schwarzschild radius, and three regimes

blocks.text: The result Schwarzschild posted on December 22, 1915 (in modern notation, with c restored).

blocks.tex: ds^2 = -\left(1 - \frac{2GM}{c^2 r}\right) c^2\, dt^2 + \left(1 - \frac{2GM}{c^2 r}\right)^{-1} dr^2 + r^2 (d\theta^2 + \sin^2\!\theta\, d\varphi^2)
blocks.caption: ds² is the squared spacetime interval, the same kind of object Minkowski wrote in 1908 but now position-dependent. G is Newton's gravitational constant, 6.674×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg². M is the total mass of the central body, the only parameter the exterior solution inherits from the source. c is the speed of light. t is the time coordinate of an inertial observer at infinity. r is the areal radial coordinate. θ and φ are the standard polar and azimuthal angles. The two functions A(r) and B(r) of the previous section have been pinned down: A(r) = 1 − 2GM/(c²r) and B(r) = 1/A(r), making A(r)·B(r) = 1. The angular part r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²) is forced by spherical symmetry and contains no surprises.

blocks.text: The combination 2GM/c² has units of length and gets its own name and symbol, the Schwarzschild radius.

blocks.caption: r_s is the Schwarzschild radius, a length built from the source parameters and fundamental constants. G is Newton's gravitational constant. M is the total mass of the central body. c is the speed of light. For the Sun, with M = 1.989×10³⁰ kg, r_s = 2.95 km, smaller than Manhattan and about 4.25 parts per million of the actual solar radius. For the Earth, with M = 5.972×10²⁴ kg, r_s = 8.87 mm, a marble. For the supermassive black hole Sgr A* at the centre of the Milky Way, with M ≈ 4.15×10⁶ solar masses, r_s ≈ 1.22×10¹⁰ m, about 17 solar radii. For the M87* black hole imaged by the Event Horizon Telescope in April 2019, with M ≈ 6.5×10⁹ solar masses, r_s ≈ 1.9×10¹³ m, about 128 astronomical units.

blocks.text: In r_s, the line element collapses to a tidier form with a single dimensionless quantity r_s/r controlling everything.

blocks.tex: ds^2 = -\left(1 - \frac{r_s}{r}\right) c^2\, dt^2 + \left(1 - \frac{r_s}{r}\right)^{-1} dr^2 + r^2 (d\theta^2 + \sin^2\!\theta\, d\varphi^2)
blocks.caption: ds² is the spacetime interval. r_s is the Schwarzschild radius from the previous formula. r is the areal radial coordinate. The dimensionless ratio r_s/r controls the deviation from flat spacetime: r_s/r → 0 (large r) is the Minkowski limit, and r_s/r → 1 (r → r_s) is the event horizon. The two metric coefficients are −g_tt = 1 − r_s/r, which is the prefactor on dt² (negative under our sign convention because t is timelike), and g_rr = 1/(1 − r_s/r), which is the prefactor on dr². The reciprocal relationship g_rr = 1/(−g_tt/c²) (after cleaning out factors of c²) is a notable feature of the Schwarzschild solution; it does not hold for the Kerr or Reissner-Nordström generalisations.

The two non-trivial Schwarzschild metric coefficients plotted against r/r_s. Drag the slider to move a vertical probe between r = r_s (horizon) and r = 6 r_s; the readout shows the local time-dilation factor (a distant clock and a stationary local clock disagree by 1/√(1 − r_s/r)) and the radial-ruler stretch factor (a distant ruler and a stationary local ruler disagree by √(1/(1 − r_s/r))). The shaded band at r < r_s is the interior of the event horizon, where the coefficient changes sign and the r coordinate becomes timelike. The three vertical markers are the horizon (r = r_s), the photon sphere (r = 1.5 r_s, where light orbits unstably), and the innermost stable circular orbit (r = 3 r_s, where massive-particle stable orbits end).

blocks.text: Three regimes are visible in the viz. At large r (r_s/r ≪ 1) the metric coefficients approach 1 and the spacetime is Minkowski. The leading correction is −g_tt ≈ 1 − r_s/r = 1 + 2Φ/c², where Φ = −GM/r is the Newtonian gravitational potential; this is how general relativity reduces to Newtonian gravity at first order in r_s/r. For Mercury at perihelion, r_s/r ≈ 5×10⁻⁸, and the deviation from Newtonian gravity is one part in 2×10⁷ on average. Section 4 shows that a million orbits of that tiny correction add up to a measurable 43 arcseconds per century. At intermediate r the photon sphere (1.5 r_s) and the ISCO (3 r_s) carve out the orbital structure that every accretion disk and Event Horizon Telescope image sits on top of. At r → r_s the coefficients do something dramatic; section 5 returns to read it correctly.

sections.heading: 4. Mercury's perihelion: 43 arcseconds per century, the first quantitative win for GR

blocks.text: Einstein worked Mercury's orbit before he worked anything else, because the problem had been the most embarrassing open question in celestial mechanics for fifty-six years. In 1859 the French astronomer Urbain Le Verrier (who had used Newtonian perturbation theory to predict the position of Neptune in 1846) completed a multi-decade reanalysis of Mercury's transit timings and found that the planet's perihelion advanced by 575 arcseconds per century. Of that, 531 arcseconds were accounted for by Newtonian perturbations from the other planets, dominated by Venus and Jupiter. The residual 44 arcseconds (later refined to 43) had no Newtonian source.

Le Verrier proposed an undiscovered planet Vulcan inside Mercury's orbit; sixty years of professional and amateur searches found nothing. Asaph Hall in 1894 tried modifying Newton's inverse-square law to inverse 1/r^(2+ε); ε ≈ 1.6×10⁻⁷ fixed Mercury and broke the Moon. Simon Newcomb's 1895 catalog of failed fixes called the residual one of the most stubborn problems in physics. Einstein knew the problem cold by the time he reached the field equations in November 1915.

The calculation in modern form. A timelike geodesic in the equatorial plane (θ = π/2) of the Schwarzschild metric has two conserved quantities along the geodesic, energy and angular momentum, because the metric does not depend on t or φ. Setting u = 1/r and using φ as the parameter, the two conservation laws combine into a single second-order orbit equation that replaces Newton's Kepler equation.

blocks.caption: u = 1/r is the inverse radial coordinate, the standard substitution Kepler-orbit problems use to linearise the equation. φ is the orbital azimuthal angle, used as the independent variable instead of time. d²u/dφ² is the second derivative of u with respect to φ. G is Newton's gravitational constant. M is the central mass. h is the orbital angular momentum per unit test-particle mass, a constant of the motion. c is the speed of light. The first two terms (the left side and the GM/h² right side) form the Newtonian Kepler orbit equation, whose bound solution is the closed ellipse Kepler found in 1609. The last term, 3GMu²/c², is the relativistic correction from the Schwarzschild metric; it has no analog in Newtonian mechanics and is what makes the orbit precess.

blocks.text: The Newtonian piece (the equation without the last term) has the bound solution u = (GM/h²)(1 + e cos φ), the standard ellipse with constant orientation. The relativistic correction is small (for Mercury, the right side's second term is about 10⁻⁷ of the first), so perturbation theory applies cleanly. To leading order the correction modifies the cosine argument from cos φ to cos((1 − δ)φ), where δ = 3(GM)²/(c h)² is a small dimensionless number. The closed Newtonian orbit becomes an ellipse whose major axis rotates by an angle Δφ per revolution.

blocks.caption: Δφ_perihelion is the angular shift of the perihelion (the orbital point of closest approach) per complete revolution, in radians. The 6π factor comes from 2π × 3 in the perturbation theory: one 2π per revolution from the cosine, times the dimensionless 3(GM)²/(c h)² shift, simplified using the Newtonian relation h² = GM·a(1 − e²) between angular momentum, semimajor axis a, and orbital eccentricity e. G is Newton's gravitational constant. M is the mass of the central body, here the Sun. c is the speed of light. a is the semimajor axis of the orbit. e is the orbital eccentricity. The combination a(1 − e²) is the semilatus rectum p, the orbital scale that controls the precession; smaller p gives larger precession.

blocks.text: Plug Mercury's numbers. GM_sun = 1.327×10²⁰ m³/s². a = 5.791×10¹⁰ m, e = 0.2056, 1 − e² = 0.9577. Per revolution: Δφ = 6π × 1.327×10²⁰ / (9×10¹⁶ × 5.791×10¹⁰ × 0.9577) ≈ 5.02×10⁻⁷ radians. Convert to a century. Mercury's orbital period is 87.97 days, and a Julian century is 36 525 days, giving 415.2 orbits per century. Total: 5.02×10⁻⁷ × 415.2 ≈ 2.08×10⁻⁴ radians, which is 43.0 arcseconds. The four-figure agreement with Le Verrier's 1859 residual is the first quantitative success of general relativity. Einstein wrote to Paul Ehrenfest that the result had given him heart palpitations for three days.

quiz.question: Mercury shows a 43 arcsec/century perihelion advance from general relativity. The same formula applied to the other inner planets gives Venus 8.6 arcsec/century, Earth 3.8 arcsec/century, Mars 1.4 arcsec/century. Which factor in the formula Δφ = 6πGM/[c²a(1 − e²)] is the dominant reason Mercury wins?
options: Mercury's small mass; lighter test particles feel relativity more strongly than heavy ones.
Mercury's high orbital speed; the precession scales with v²/c².

Mercury's small semilatus rectum a(1 − e²); the precession scales as the inverse of this quantity, and Mercury has the smallest a of any planet plus an unusually high eccentricity, giving it the smallest a(1 − e²) and thus the largest precession.
Mercury's frequent perihelion passages; the precession per century scales with the number of orbits per century.
quiz.explanation: The formula depends on the central mass M (the same Sun for every planet) and the orbiting planet's geometry through the semilatus rectum p = a(1 − e²). The orbiting planet's own mass does not appear, because the Schwarzschild solution is vacuum and the test particle's mass cancels out of the geodesic equation (a relativistic version of the Newtonian fact that gravity is independent of inertial mass). Mercury wins on geometry: it has the smallest a (smallest by far among the four inner planets) and a high enough eccentricity (e = 0.206) to shrink (1 − e²) further. Venus is next at a = 1.082×10¹¹ m but with nearly zero eccentricity, dropping its precession to 8.6 arcsec/century. Option (a) misidentifies mass dependence; the equivalence principle removes it. Option (b) names an effect (v²/c² ~ GM/(c²a) is roughly the size of the per-orbit correction) but misses that the question asks which factor in the formula, and v² does not appear explicitly. Option (d) double-counts: the per-orbit shift and the orbits-per-century are both in the calculation, but the formula in the question already gives the per-orbit shift, and Mercury's edge there is the semilatus rectum, not the orbital frequency.

sections.heading: 5. The horizon: a coordinate singularity that took 44 years to read correctly

blocks.text: Schwarzschild's original coordinates do something dramatic at r = r_s. The metric coefficient −g_tt = 1 − r_s/r vanishes (a clock at rest there ticks infinitely slowly to a distant observer) and g_rr = 1/(1 − r_s/r) diverges (radial rulers stretch without bound). For decades the natural reading was that r = r_s was a real boundary of the geometry, a singular surface beyond which spacetime ended. Einstein himself believed this through the 1930s, and his 1939 Annals of Mathematics paper argued that no physically realistic process could produce a body small enough for r = r_s to lie outside its surface. He died in 1955 still uneasy about black holes.

The reading was wrong, and the fix took 44 years. The decisive distinction was between a coordinate singularity (an artifact of the particular chart, like the longitude blow-up at the north pole) and a curvature singularity (a real breakdown of the geometry). The test is a curvature invariant: a scalar built from the Riemann curvature tensor whose value is the same in every chart. The simplest one is the Kretschmann scalar, the full contraction of the Riemann tensor with itself.

blocks.caption: K is the Kretschmann scalar, a single real number defined at each point of spacetime. R_μνρσ is the Riemann curvature tensor, with one upper and three lower indices in its original definition. R^μνρσ is the same tensor with all four indices raised using the inverse metric g^μν. The repeated index pairs (μ, ν, ρ, σ) are summed under the Einstein convention, so K is a sum of (Riemann)² components, and is therefore a curvature invariant: its value at a point depends on the geometry but not on the chosen coordinates. K = 0 everywhere identifies flat (Minkowski) spacetime. K > 0 indicates curvature; K finite at a point means the geometry is smooth there even if the metric components in a particular chart blow up.

blocks.text: For the Schwarzschild metric the Kretschmann scalar evaluates to a function of r alone.

blocks.caption: K_Schwarzschild is the Kretschmann scalar evaluated on the Schwarzschild metric, in SI units of m⁻⁴. G is Newton's gravitational constant. M is the central mass. c is the speed of light. r is the areal radial coordinate. The dependence is r⁻⁶, with no dependence on t, θ, or φ (by the spherical symmetry and time independence of the metric). At r = r_s = 2GM/c² the scalar evaluates to 3c⁸/(4G⁴M⁴), perfectly finite: the geometry is smooth at the horizon. At r → 0 the scalar diverges as r⁻⁶, the genuine curvature singularity at the centre of every Schwarzschild black hole.

blocks.text: Once the diagnosis was made the coordinates followed. Arthur Eddington in 1924 wrote down v = t + r* (with r* = r + r_s ln|r/r_s − 1|, what Tullio Regge and John Wheeler later called the tortoise coordinate) that made the line element manifestly regular at r = r_s. David Finkelstein in 1958, working two doors from Wheeler at Princeton, rediscovered Eddington's coordinates independently and noticed what they were saying: at r = r_s the light cones tip over. Outside, future-directed null directions point both ways in r; inside, both point only inward to decreasing r. The event horizon is not a wall but a one-way membrane, and once you have crossed it, escape is forbidden not by a force but by the local geometry of time.

Martin Kruskal at Princeton and George Szekeres in Adelaide independently published the maximal analytic extension in 1960, with a coordinate system (U, V) that covers two asymptotically flat universes connected by a non-traversable wormhole (the Einstein-Rosen bridge of 1935), with two horizons and the r = 0 singularity all visible in a single chart. The black hole is the lower-future region of the Kruskal diagram; outside is asymptotically flat; the past-future symmetry of the maximal extension is broken in nature only because realistic black holes form by collapse rather than existing eternally. John Wheeler coined the name 'black hole' at a 1967 NASA conference, fifty-one years after Schwarzschild had written down its exterior metric without knowing what it described. The line element from the Eastern Front contained the entire object; the discipline took two generations to develop the geometric vocabulary, Riemann's 1854 distinction between intrinsic geometry and coordinate descriptions, to read it correctly. A complete answer, posted from a trench in 1916, can wait 44 years for an audience prepared to receive it.

finalQuiz.question: The Schwarzschild metric is a single algebraic formula with one free parameter (the mass M), and the same formula yields the Newtonian gravitational potential at large r, Mercury's 43 arcsec/century perihelion precession at intermediate r, and the event horizon at r = r_s. Which of the following best captures why this works and what it teaches?
options: Birkhoff's theorem makes the uniqueness of the Schwarzschild metric a precise mathematical statement: spherical symmetry plus vacuum forces this one solution, with the total mass M as the only data inherited from the source. The three regimes (weak field, intermediate, horizon) are different limits of the same exact formula, not three independent predictions that happen to agree.
The Schwarzschild metric is an approximation that breaks down at the horizon, where a separate strong-field theory must take over.

The Newtonian limit, the perihelion precession, and the event horizon are three independent predictions of general relativity, derived from three different metrics that happen to agree in the spherical-vacuum case.
The mass M and the angular momentum J both appear in the Schwarzschild metric, and varying J between zero and the extremal Kerr value tunes the metric between the three regimes.
finalQuiz.explanation: Birkhoff's theorem (1923), introduced in section 2's quiz, makes the uniqueness statement precise: once you assume spherical symmetry and vacuum, the only data from the source that survives in the exterior is the total mass M, and the one resulting metric is the Schwarzschild solution at every radius outside the source. The Newtonian potential is the leading-order behavior of −g_tt at large r/r_s (section 3). Mercury's 43 arcsec/century is the next-to-leading-order behavior of the geodesic equation at intermediate r/r_s (section 4). The event horizon's coordinate singularity is the behavior of the same metric at r/r_s = 1, smoothed by a chart change (section 5). One formula, one parameter, three limits. Option (b) is exactly wrong: section 5's Kretschmann-scalar argument and Kruskal's 1960 maximal extension show the solution is well-behaved at r = r_s; only at r = 0 does the geometry break down. Option (c) misunderstands the architecture; the three predictions are limits of one solution. Option (d) confuses Schwarzschild with Kerr; Schwarzschild has only M, and angular momentum requires the Kerr metric (Roy Kerr, 1963).
